en.extract:
The green imperial pigeon (Ducula aenea) is a large forest pigeon. The large range extends from Nepal, southern India and Sri Lanka eastwards to southern China, Indonesia and the Philippines.


Taxonomy
In 1760 the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson included a description of the green imperial pigeon in his six volume Ornithologie. He used the French name Le pigeon ramier des Moluques and the Latin Palumbus moluccensis. Although Brisson coined Latin names, these do not conform to the binomial system and are not recognised by the International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature. When in 1766 the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus updated his Systema Naturae for the twelfth edition, he added 240 species that had been previously described by Brisson. One of these was the green imperial pigeon which he placed with all the other pigeons in the genus Columba. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the binomial name Columba aenea and cited Brisson's work. Brisson believed his specimen had come from the Maluku Islands but the species does not occur there and in 1918 the type locality was designated as the island of Flores in Indonesia. The specific name aenea is from the Latin aeneus meaning "of a bronze colour" or "coppery". This species is now placed in the genus Ducula that was introduced by the English naturalist Brian Houghton Hodgson in 1836.
Eleven subspecies are recognised:

D. a. sylvatica (Tickell, 1833) – Indian green imperial pigeon – north India and Nepal to south China to Thailand, and Indochina
D. a. pusilla (Blyth, 1849) – south India, Sri Lanka
D. a. andamanica Abdulali, 1964 – Andaman Islands
D. a. consobrina (Salvadori, 1887) – islands of West Sumatra except Enggano Island
D. a. polia (Oberholser, 1917) – Sunda green imperial pigeon – Malay Peninsula to Greater and Lesser Sunda Islands
D. a. palawanensis (Blasius, W, 1888)	Banggi Island (north Borneo) to Palawan and nearby islands (southwest Philippines)
D. a. fugaensis (Hachisuka, 1930) – north Philippines (Calayan, Camiguin and Fuga Island)
D. a. nuchalis (Cabanis, 1882) – Maroon-naped imperial pigeon – north Luzon (north Philippines)
D. a. aenea (Linnaeus, 1766) – Nominate subspecies – Philippines (other than northern Luzon and Palawan)
D. a. intermedia (Meyer, AB & Wiglesworth, 1894) – Talaud imperial pigeon – Talaud Islands and Sangihe Islands
D. a. paulina (Bonaparte, 1854) – Chestnut-naped imperial pigeon – Sulawesi, Togian, Banggai and Sula Islands
The subspecies D. a. oenothorax is sometimes treated as a distinct species, the Enggano imperial pigeon (Ducula oenothorax). The Nicobar imperial pigeon (Ducula nicobarica) was formerly treated as conspecific.


Description
The green imperial pigeon is a large, plump pigeon, 45 centimetres (18 in) in length. Its back, wings and tail are metallic green. The head and underparts are white, apart from maroon undertail coverts. Sexes are similar. The bird's call is deep and resonant, and is often the first indication of the presence of this treetop species.


Distribution and habitat

This is a forest species which is a widespread resident breeding bird in tropical southern Asia from Nepal and India east to Indonesia. It has several subspecies, including the distinctive Celebes form, chestnut-naped imperial pigeon (Ducula aenea paulina).


Behaviour and ecology
This is an arboreal dove, feeding on plant material in the tree canopy. Its flight is fast and direct, with the regular beats and an occasional sharp flick of the wings which are characteristic of pigeons in general. It builds a stick nest in a tree and lays a single white egg. The birds are not very gregarious, but will form small flocks.


Gallery


References


External links

The '''green imperial pigeon''' ('''''Ducula aenea''''') is a large forest [[Dove|pigeon]]. The large range extends from Nepal, southern India and Sri Lanka eastwards to southern China, Indonesia and the Philippines.

==Taxonomy==
In 1760 the French zoologist [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]] included a description of the green imperial pigeon in his six volume ''Ornithologie''. He used the French name ''Le pigeon ramier des Moluques'' and the Latin ''Palumbus moluccensis''.<ref name=brisson>{{ cite book | last=Brisson | first=Mathurin Jacques | author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson | year=1760 | title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode contenant la division des oiseaux en ordres, sections, genres, especes & leurs variétés | volume=1 | language=fr, la | place=Paris | publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche | pages=148–149, Plate 13 fig 2 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36010576 }} The two stars (**) at the start of the section indicates that Brisson based his description on the examination of a specimen.</ref> Although Brisson coined Latin names, these do not conform to the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial system]] and are not recognised by the [[International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature]].<ref name=allen>{{cite journal |last=Allen | first=J.A. | author-link=Joel Asaph Allen | year=1910 | title=Collation of Brisson's genera of birds with those of Linnaeus |journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History |volume=28 | pages=317–335 | hdl=2246/678 }}</ref> When in 1766 the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] updated his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' for the [[12th edition of Systema Naturae|twelfth edition]], he added 240 species that had been previously described by Brisson.<ref name=allen/> One of these was the green imperial pigeon which he placed with all the other pigeons in the genus ''[[Columba (genus)|Columba]]''. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the [[binomial name]] ''Columba aenea'' and cited Brisson's work.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1766 | title=Systema naturae: per regna tria natura, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=12th | volume=1, Part 1 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii  | place=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=la | page=283 | url=http://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/42946305 }}</ref> Brisson believed his specimen had come from the [[Maluku Islands]] but the species does not occur there and in 1918 the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] was designated as the island of [[Flores]] in [[Indonesia]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Hartert | first1=Ernst | author1-link=Ernst Hartert | last2=Goodson | first2=Arthur Thomas | year=1918 | title=Notes on pigeons | journal=Novitates Zoologicae | volume=25 | pages=346–358 [346] | doi=10.5962/bhl.part.29771 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/3857180 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1937 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=3 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=46 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14477761 }}</ref> The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]] ''aenea'' is from the Latin ''aeneus'' meaning "of a bronze colour" or "coppery".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=33 }}</ref> This species is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Ducula]]'' that was introduced by the English naturalist [[Brian Houghton Hodgson]] in 1836.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Hodgson | first=Brian Houghton | author-link=Brian Houghton Hodgson | year=1836 | title=Notices of the ornithology of Nepal | journal=Asiatic Researches, or, Transactions of the Society Instituted in Bengal | volume=19 | pages=143–192 [160] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/43118053 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | year=2020 | title=Pigeons | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/pigeons/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=13 March 2020 }}</ref>

Eleven [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''D. a. sylvatica'' ([[Samuel Tickell|Tickell]], 1833) – Indian green imperial pigeon<ref>{{Cite web|title=Indian green Imperial-Pigeon (Ducula aenea sylvatica) - zootierliste.de|url=https://zootierliste.de/en/index.php?klasse=2&ordnung=217&familie=21706&art=2120125|access-date=2021-10-18|website=zootierliste.de}}</ref> – north India and Nepal to south China to Thailand, and Indochina
* ''D. a. pusilla'' ([[Edward Blyth|Blyth]], 1849) – south India, Sri Lanka
* ''D. a. andamanica'' [[Humayun Abdulali|Abdulali]], 1964 – [[Andaman Islands]]
* ''D. a. consobrina'' ([[Tommaso Salvadori|Salvadori]], 1887) – islands of [[West Sumatra]] except [[Enggano Island]]
* ''D. a. polia'' ([[Harry C. Oberholser|Oberholser]], 1917) – Sunda green imperial pigeon<ref>{{Cite web|title=Sunda green imperial-pigeon (Ducula aenea polia) - zootierliste.de|url=https://zootierliste.de/en/index.php?klasse=2&ordnung=217&familie=21706&art=55009118|access-date=2021-10-18|website=zootierliste.de}}</ref> – [[Malay Peninsula]] to [[Greater Sunda Islands|Greater]] and [[Lesser Sunda Islands]]
* ''D. a. palawanensis'' ([[August Wilhelm Heinrich Blasius|Blasius, W]], 1888)	[[Banggi Island]] (north Borneo) to [[Palawan (island)|Palawan]] and nearby islands (southwest Philippines)
* ''D. a. fugaensis'' ([[Masauji Hachisuka|Hachisuka]], 1930) – north Philippines ([[Calayan, Cagayan|Calayan]], [[Camiguin]] and [[Fuga Island]])
* ''D. a. nuchalis'' ([[Jean Cabanis|Cabanis]], 1882) – Maroon-naped imperial pigeon<ref>{{Cite web|title=Maroon-naped imperial pigeon (D. a. nuchalis) - Avibase|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=7B76F363&sec=summary&ssver=1|access-date=2021-10-18|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> – north [[Luzon]] (north Philippines)
* ''D. a. aenea'' (Linnaeus, 1766) – Nominate subspecies – Philippines (other than northern Luzon and Palawan)
* ''D. a. intermedia'' ([[Adolf Bernhard Meyer|Meyer, AB]] & [[Lionel William Wiglesworth|Wiglesworth]], 1894) – Talaud imperial pigeon<ref>{{Cite web|title=Talaud imperial pigeon (D. a. intermedia) - Avibase|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=BF32B01F&sec=summary&ssver=1|access-date=2021-10-18|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> – [[Talaud Islands]] and [[Sangihe Islands]]
* ''D. a. paulina'' ([[Charles Lucien Bonaparte|Bonaparte]], 1854) – Chestnut-naped imperial pigeon<ref>{{cite web |title=ZootierlisteHomepage |url=https://zootierliste.de/en/index.php?klasse=2&ordnung=217&familie=21706&art=2120115 |website=zootierliste.de |access-date=14 August 2022}}</ref> – [[Sulawesi]], [[Togian Islands|Togian]], [[Banggai Islands Regency|Banggai]] and [[Sula Islands Regency|Sula Islands]]

The subspecies ''D. a. oenothorax'' is sometimes treated as a distinct species, the Enggano imperial pigeon (''Ducula oenothorax'').<ref>{{cite web | last1=del Hoyo | first1=J. | last2=Collar | first2=N. | last3=Kirwan | first3=G.M. | last4=Garcia | first4=E.F.J. | year=2020 | title=Enggano Imperial-pigeon (''Ducula oenothorax'') | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | editor5-last=de Juana | editor5-first=E. | work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | publisher=Lynx Edicions | url=https://www.hbw.com/node/467157 | access-date=14 March 2020 | url-access=subscription }}</ref> The [[Nicobar imperial pigeon]] (''Ducula nicobarica'') was formerly treated as [[conspecific]].<ref name=ioc/><ref name=hbw>{{cite web | last1=Baptista | first1=L.F. | last2=Trail | first2=P.W. | last3=Horblit | first3=H.M. | last4=Kirwan | first4=G.M. | last5=Boesman | first5=P. | last6=Garcia | first6=E.F.J. | year=2020 | title=Green Imperial-pigeon (''Ducula aenea'') | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | editor5-last=de Juana | editor5-first=E. | work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | publisher=Lynx Edicions | url=https://www.hbw.com/node/54369 | access-date=14 March 2020 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Description==
The green imperial pigeon is a large, plump pigeon, {{convert|45|cm}} in length. Its back, wings and tail are metallic green. The head and underparts are white, apart from maroon undertail coverts. Sexes are similar. The bird's call is deep and resonant, and is often the first indication of the presence of this treetop species.

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Ducula aenea -San Diego Zoo, California, USA-8a.jpg|right|thumb|Feeding at the [[San Diego Zoo]], USA]]
This is a forest species which is a widespread resident breeding bird in tropical southern [[Asia]] from [[Nepal]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=Maden |first=Kamal |title=Rare wild pigeon sighted in Nepal |date=28 November 2020 |url=https://www.nepalitimes.com/latest/rare-wild-pigeon-sighted-in-nepal/ |access-date=2020-11-30 |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-11-27 |title=Green pigeon spotted in Jhapa |url=http://thehimalayantimes.com/?p=706053 |access-date=2020-11-30 |website=The Himalayan Times |language=en-US}}</ref> and [[India]] east to [[Indonesia]]. It has several subspecies, including the distinctive [[Sulawesi|Celebes]] form, chestnut-naped imperial pigeon (''Ducula aenea paulina'').

==Behaviour and ecology==
This is an [[Arboreal locomotion|arboreal]] dove, feeding on plant material in the tree canopy. Its flight is fast and direct, with the regular beats and an occasional sharp flick of the wings which are characteristic of pigeons in general. It builds a stick nest in a tree and lays a single white [[egg (biology)|egg]]. The birds are not very gregarious, but will form small flocks.

zh.extract:
大綠鳩（学名：Ducula aenea）为鸠鸽科皇鸠属的鸟类，俗名绿南鸠、大绿鸠。分布于沿綠皇鳩缘向南、西至巴基斯坦、东至菲律宾群岛、南抵印度、印度尼西亚以及中国大陆的广东、海南、云南等地，主要栖息于丘陵的阔叶林以及河谷的次生林、平原或丘陵地带居民点附近的榕树、橄榄树等。该物种的模式产地在印度尼西亚弗洛勒斯。


保护
中国国家重点保护动物等级：二级


亚种
绿皇鸠广东亚种（学名：Ducula aenea kwantungensis）。在中国大陆，分布于广东、海南等地。该物种的模式产地在广东罗浮山一带。
绿皇鸠云南亚种（学名：Ducula aenea sylvatica）。分布于尼泊尔、孟加拉、印度、缅甸、泰国、老挝、越南、柬埔寨以及中国大陆的云南等地。该物种的模式产地在印度。
